title: How Interest Rate Changes Affect Credit Card Spending
summary: Federal Reserve Bank of Boston economists Falk Bräuning and Joanna Stavins find that a 1 percentage point increase in credit card APR reduces consumer spending by 8.7 percent in the following month. Using supervisory data covering nearly 80 percent of US credit card accounts from 2016–2025, they employ a regression kink design exploiting contractual APR ceilings to isolate causal effects. The research reveals that revolvers (balance carriers) and lower-credit-score borrowers drive the spending response, reducing spending by 15 percent and 18 percent respectively per 1 percentage point APR increase, while transactors and higher-credit-score borrowers show minimal spending changes but do reduce outstanding balances.
key_points: A 1 percentage point increase in credit card APR reduces credit card spending by approximately 8.7 percent in the following month on average
Revolvers (those carrying month-to-month balances) reduce spending by about 15 percent per 1 percentage point APR increase, nearly double the overall average effect

Lower-credit-score borrowers reduce spending by approximately 18 percent per 1 percentage point APR increase, while higher-credit-score borrowers maintain spending but reduce balances by about 7 percent
Prime rate equals the federal funds rate plus 3 percentage points, and credit card APRs typically equal the prime rate plus an individual margin set at account opening
Credit card contracts typically include a maximum APR ceiling of 29.99 percent, creating a kink exploited in the regression kink design methodology
In 2022, US consumers made $5.83 trillion in credit card purchases, representing approximately 20 percent of all consumer spending
why_it_matters: This research quantifies a key transmission mechanism for monetary policy: credit card rates. With credit cards representing roughly 20 percent of consumer spending, the finding that a 1 percentage point rate increase reduces spending by 8.7 percent (with a two-month lag for full effect) provides a concrete estimate of how Fed rate decisions flow through to household consumption. The asymmetric impact—concentrated among revolvers and lower-credit-score borrowers—suggests monetary tightening disproportionately burdens financially constrained households.
macro_context: The Federal Reserve's Federal Open Market Committee sets the federal funds rate, which flows through to the prime rate within approximately one month. The prime rate plus individual margins determines credit card APRs for most accounts. This study provides empirical estimates of how these rate changes affect consumer spending, with the largest aggregate effects occurring approximately two months after the rate change, consistent with the time required for card rates to adjust and consumers to respond.
market_structure_context: Credit card market structure is characterized by variable APRs tied to the prime rate, with margins varying significantly by creditworthiness (19-20 percentage points for lower scores vs. 11-12 for excellent credit). The typical 29.99 percent contractual ceiling creates a kink that constrains rate pass-through at high-rate levels. The $5.83 trillion in 2022 credit card volume represents approximately 20 percent of consumer spending, making this market segment material for monetary policy transmission.
notes: Source is a peer-reviewed research publication from the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston with clear methodology (regression kink design) and comprehensive data coverage (80 percent of US credit card accounts 2016–2025). The causal identification strategy using contractual APR ceilings is rigorous. Note: published_at metadata shows 2026-03-25, which appears to be a future date relative to the current date, but this is from the source metadata and should be treated as authoritative per instructions. The research does not capture substitution effects to other payment methods or credit forms.
